news_summary: Today’s human rights news highlights strengthened protections for education in Zambia, efforts to ensure fair compensation in digital journalism, renewed global solidarity for refugees, and ongoing commitment to addressing challenges within the Human Rights Council.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en avant le renforcement des mesures de protection en faveur de l'éducation en Zambie, les efforts visant à garantir une rémunération équitable dans le journalisme numérique, le regain de solidarité mondiale envers les réfugiés, ainsi que l'engagement continu à relever les défis au sein du Conseil des droits de l'homme.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan el refuerzo de las medidas de protección de la educación en Zambia, los esfuerzos por garantizar una remuneración justa en el periodismo digital, la renovada solidaridad mundial con los refugiados y el compromiso constante para abordar los retos que se plantean en el Consejo de Derechos Humanos.

articles.title: A new law in Zambia makes free education much harder for future governments to take away
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM There’s a particular kind of law that changes nothing overnight. The classrooms look the same the morning after it passes. The teachers haven’t changed. The children getting on buses are the same children who got on buses yesterday. But something has shifted underneath: what was once a promise has […]
The post A new law in Zambia makes free education much harder for future governments to take away first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

There’s a particular kind of law that changes nothing overnight. The classrooms look the same the morning after it passes. The teachers haven’t changed. The children getting on buses are the same children who got on buses yesterday. But something has shifted underneath: what was once a promise has become a right.

Zambia passed one of those laws this month. President Hakainde Hichilema signed the Education (Amendment) Act 2026, writing free public education into the country’s legal framework for every child from early childhood through secondary school. The policy has existed since 2022. But now, it cannot be unwound by the next government. A future administration would need parliamentary approval to reverse it.

From policy to protection

When Zambia abolished school fees in 2022, the results were immediate. More than 2.6 million children returned to school, according to government figures. For many families, the fee had been the barrier. Remove it, and the children show up.

But a government policy is only as durable as the government that holds it. Vice-President Mutale Nalumango framed the new legislation as deliberate insulation against exactly that risk: access to education should not depend on the priorities of whichever administration happens to be in power. A legal obligation is a different thing from a political one. That distinction may prove to be the law’s most lasting contribution.

The scale of what’s already changed

The education figures Zambia has released in recent years are striking. In 2025, the country recorded a 70 percent Grade 12 pass rate, its highest on record. The government has also expanded classroom construction, recruited additional teachers, and grown school feeding programs that now support millions of learners.

Officials point to these figures as evidence that access and outcomes can improve together. Education researchers are more measured: the real test is whether investment continues to keep pace with enrolment as more children enter a system that was already under pressure before 2022.

What the rest of the continent’s experience suggests

Zambia is not the first African country to pursue free education at scale. Ghana’s Free Senior High School program brought a surge in enrolment after 2017, but also created enough overcrowding that authorities introduced a double-track system to manage the load. When Kenya abolished primary school fees in 2003, millions of new pupils arrived almost overnight. Tanzania, Sierra Leone, Malawi, and South Africa have all attempted different versions of fee-free schooling, each confronting the same underlying tension: getting children through the door is the easier half of the problem.

The harder half is what happens once they’re inside. Class sizes, teacher quality, whether rural communities are served as well as urban ones: these are the variables that determine whether a legal right to education becomes a meaningful one.

What comes next

The law is a floor, not a ceiling. Zambia has guaranteed the right. What it builds on top of that guarantee will determine whether 2026 is remembered as a turning point or a paperwork milestone.

For the 2.6 million children who came back to school after 2022, the question is less abstract. They are already there. The new law is, among other things, a promise to them that the door will stay open.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Zambia passing a law that legally guarantees free public education for all children, protecting this right from being revoked by future governments. This represents a significant, durable policy change with broad societal impact, ensuring access to education for millions and promoting equality and opportunity. The article provides detailed context on the law's implications, past policy effects, and challenges ahead, making it a substantive and inspiring story of human rights progress.
articles.personality_title: Zambia makes free education a protected legal right for all children
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2022, Zambia removed school fees, allowing millions of children to return to school. However, this was a government policy that could have been changed by future leaders.

**What happened** – In 2026, Zambia passed a new law called the Education (Amendment) Act. This law makes free public education from early childhood to secondary school a legal right. President Hakainde Hichilema signed it, meaning future governments cannot remove free education without approval from parliament.

**Impact** – This law changes free education from a promise into a protected right. Over 2.6 million children have already benefited since fees were removed. Now, the law helps ensure that no government can easily take away this benefit. It supports fairness by making sure education access does not depend on political changes.

**What's next step** – Zambia will need to continue investing in schools, teachers, and programs like school feeding to handle more students and improve quality. The law sets a strong base, but what happens inside classrooms will decide if this change truly improves education for all.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Zambia’s new law legally guarantees free education for every child, protecting this right from future political changes and supporting millions of learners.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Zambie garantit légalement l’éducation gratuite pour tous les enfants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2022, la Zambie a supprimé les frais scolaires, permettant à des millions d’enfants de retourner à l’école. Cependant, cette mesure était une politique gouvernementale qui aurait pu être modifiée par les futurs dirigeants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, la Zambie a adopté une nouvelle loi appelée la Loi (amendement) sur l’éducation. Cette loi fait de l’éducation publique gratuite, de la petite enfance au secondaire, un droit légal. Le président Hakainde Hichilema l’a signée, ce qui signifie que les gouvernements futurs ne peuvent pas supprimer la gratuité sans l’accord du parlement.

**Impact** – Cette loi transforme la gratuité de l’éducation d’une promesse en un droit protégé. Plus de 2,6 millions d’enfants en ont déjà bénéficié depuis la suppression des frais. La loi garantit qu’aucun gouvernement ne peut facilement retirer cet avantage, assurant ainsi l’égalité d’accès à l’éducation malgré les changements politiques.

**Prochaine étape** – La Zambie devra continuer à investir dans les écoles, les enseignants et les programmes comme la cantine scolaire pour accueillir plus d’élèves et améliorer la qualité. La loi pose une base solide, mais ce qui se passe dans les classes décidera si ce changement améliore vraiment l’éducation pour tous.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle loi en Zambie garantit légalement l’éducation gratuite pour chaque enfant, protégeant ce droit contre les changements politiques futurs et aidant des millions d’élèves.
articles.personality_title_es: Zambia asegura legalmente la educación gratuita para todos los niños
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2022, Zambia eliminó las cuotas escolares, permitiendo que millones de niños regresaran a la escuela. Sin embargo, esta era una política del gobierno que podría ser cambiada por futuros líderes.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, Zambia aprobó una nueva ley llamada Ley de Enmienda Educativa. Esta ley convierte la educación pública gratuita desde la primera infancia hasta la secundaria en un derecho legal. El presidente Hakainde Hichilema la firmó, lo que significa que futuros gobiernos no pueden eliminar la educación gratuita sin la aprobación del parlamento.

**Impacto** – Esta ley transforma la educación gratuita de una promesa a un derecho protegido. Más de 2.6 millones de niños ya se han beneficiado desde que se eliminaron las cuotas. La ley asegura que ningún gobierno pueda quitar fácilmente este beneficio, garantizando el acceso a la educación sin importar los cambios políticos.

**Próximo paso** – Zambia deberá seguir invirtiendo en escuelas, maestros y programas como la alimentación escolar para manejar más estudiantes y mejorar la calidad. La ley establece una base fuerte, pero lo que pase en las aulas decidirá si este cambio mejora realmente la educación para todos.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva ley de Zambia garantiza legalmente la educación gratuita para todos los niños, protegiendo este derecho de cambios políticos futuros y apoyando a millones de estudiantes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy open school gate made of intertwined books and pencils, standing firmly on a sunlit Zambian landscape with a winding path leading to a vibrant cluster of simple school buildings filled with glowing windows, surrounded by silhouettes of joyful children’s shadows playing and learning, symbolizing the legal protection of free education that keeps the door open for all.

articles.title: UNESCO launches consultation on fair payment for news in the digital age
articles.summary: UN cultural agency UNESCO has launched a global consultation process to inform its Draft Guidance on Fair Compensation for News, particularly as online platforms and artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly rely on journalistic content.
articles.content:
The initiative comes “at a time when securing the sustainability of news media is more urgent than ever to protect the future of journalism and safeguard information integrity,” the agency said.

The text under consultation outlines disruptions to the media landscape, including decreased funding for public-interest journalism, the contraction or closure of local and community news organisations, and other challenges that indicate “a fundamental and ongoing change in the structure of the information economy.”

Few players, wide influence

“A small number of large, multinational digital platforms and AI actors now occupy a central intermediary role between media and the public, shaping content discovery, influencing the conditions through which journalism reaches audiences, and mediating access to digital advertising markets in ways that have materially altered the economic conditions in which journalism operates,” it said.

UNESCO is seeking feedback from governments, regulatory authorities, media, civil society, academia and other stakeholders through 30 July.

Through an online survey, respondents can share their inputs and perspectives on how the document “can be improved to achieve the aim of safeguarding freedom of expression, strengthening media viability, and supporting the future of independent journalism in the context of growing digital platforms and AI actors.”

Submissions can be in English, French or Spanish.

Ahead of the deadline, three online regional roundtables will be convened to facilitate dialogue and input from people in Asia-Pacific and the Arab States, Africa and Europe, and the Americas and the Caribbean.

Supporting media diversity

The draft guidance builds on UNESCO’s Guidelines for the Governance of Digital Platforms, issued in 2023, which call for supporting media sustainability, diversity, and pluralism.

It is also informed by the agency’s work on generative AI governance and human rights impact assessments, as well as global principles promoting fair compensation for journalism.

The initiative further reinforces UNESCO’s broader efforts to strengthen media independence, sustainability and viability.

The final draft of the Guidance will be published later this year, alongside a report summarizing the key insights and contributions received.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes UNESCO's global consultation initiative aimed at ensuring fair compensation for news in the digital age, addressing the sustainability of journalism and safeguarding freedom of expression. This has broad societal significance as it supports independent journalism, media diversity, and information integrity, which are crucial for informed societies and democratic processes. The article provides substantial context about the challenges faced by journalism and the inclusive consultation process, indicating a meaningful positive impact.
articles.personality_title: UNESCO invites global input on fair pay for news in the digital era
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Journalism is changing because many people now get news online, and big tech companies and artificial intelligence (AI) play a big role in sharing news. This has made it harder for news organizations to earn money and keep producing reliable news.

**What happened** – UNESCO, a United Nations agency focused on culture and education, started a worldwide consultation to help create fair rules for paying news producers. They want to hear from governments, media groups, and regular people by July 30. The consultation includes an online survey and regional online meetings covering Asia-Pacific, Africa, Europe, the Americas, and the Caribbean.

**Impact** – This effort is important because it aims to protect the future of independent journalism. When news organizations get fair payment, they can continue providing trustworthy information. This helps keep news diverse and supports free speech, which is important for healthy communities and democracies.

**What's next step** – UNESCO will gather all the feedback and publish a final guidance document later this year. This document will suggest how to make sure news creators get fair pay and how digital platforms and AI should support journalism.

**One-sentence takeaway** – UNESCO’s global consultation seeks ideas to ensure fair payment for news, helping journalism stay strong and independent in the digital age.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UNESCO lance une consultation mondiale sur une rémunération équitable des actualités à l’ère numérique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le journalisme évolue car beaucoup de personnes consultent les informations en ligne, où les grandes entreprises technologiques et l’intelligence artificielle (IA) jouent un rôle important. Cela complique la vie des médias pour gagner de l’argent et continuer à fournir des informations fiables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’UNESCO, une agence culturelle des Nations Unies, a lancé une consultation mondiale pour créer des règles justes sur la rémunération des producteurs d’actualités. Elle souhaite recevoir des avis des gouvernements, des médias et du public avant le 30 juillet. La consultation comprend un sondage en ligne et des réunions régionales en ligne pour l’Asie-Pacifique, l’Afrique, l’Europe, les Amériques et les Caraïbes.

**Impact** – Cette initiative est importante car elle vise à protéger l’avenir du journalisme indépendant. Quand les médias sont justement payés, ils peuvent continuer à fournir des informations fiables. Cela permet de garder la diversité des médias et soutient la liberté d’expression, essentielle pour des sociétés et des démocraties saines.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UNESCO rassemblera tous les retours et publiera un guide final cette année, proposant des moyens d’assurer une rémunération équitable des créateurs d’actualités et de définir le rôle des plateformes numériques et de l’IA.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La consultation mondiale de l’UNESCO recueille des idées pour garantir une rémunération juste des actualités, afin de soutenir un journalisme fort et indépendant à l’ère numérique.
articles.personality_title_es: La UNESCO lanza consulta global sobre pago justo por noticias en la era digital
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El periodismo está cambiando porque muchas personas ahora acceden a las noticias en línea, donde las grandes empresas tecnológicas y la inteligencia artificial (IA) tienen un papel importante. Esto ha dificultado que los medios ganen dinero y sigan produciendo noticias confiables.

**Qué sucedió** – La UNESCO, una agencia cultural de las Naciones Unidas, inició una consulta mundial para ayudar a crear reglas justas sobre el pago a los productores de noticias. Quieren recibir opiniones de gobiernos, medios y público antes del 30 de julio. La consulta incluye una encuesta en línea y reuniones regionales virtuales para Asia-Pacífico, África, Europa, América y el Caribe.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque busca proteger el futuro del periodismo independiente. Cuando los medios reciben un pago justo, pueden seguir ofreciendo información confiable. Esto ayuda a mantener la diversidad de noticias y apoya la libertad de expresión, esencial para comunidades y democracias saludables.

**Próximo paso** – La UNESCO recopilará todas las opiniones y publicará una guía final este año. Este documento propondrá cómo asegurar un pago justo a los creadores de noticias y cómo las plataformas digitales y la IA deben apoyar al periodismo.

**Frase clave** – La consulta global de la UNESCO busca ideas para garantizar un pago justo por las noticias, ayudando a que el periodismo siga fuerte e independiente en la era digital.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, open book glowing softly at the center, surrounded by diverse, interconnected digital threads and abstract shapes representing news media and AI platforms, with multiple hands of different sizes and colors gently reaching toward the book to symbolize collaboration and fair support for independent journalism in the digital age.

articles.title: World Refugee Day: UN calls for renewed commitment and solidarity
articles.summary: The UN High Commissioner for Refugees has called on the international community to strengthen support for the nearly 42 million people worldwide who have fled their home countries to escape conflict, violence or persecution.
articles.content:
Barham Salih highlighted the contributions refugees make to their host communities as workers, students, neighbours, artists, athletes, entrepreneurs and leaders.

“Given the opportunity, they rebuild their lives and help strengthen the societies around them,” he said ahead of World Refugee Day, observed annually on 20 June.

Turbulent times

UN Secretary-General António Guterres issued a message calling for stronger support for people forced to flee and the communities that have welcomed them.

Tweet URL

“As divisions deepen across our world, new and protracted conflicts are compelling millions of women, children and men to seek safety far from home,” he said.

He stressed that “these turbulent times” call for renewed solidarity and robust action to protect refugees.

A difficult choice

“Fleeing home to seek safety is one of the hardest choices anyone can make. I know that from personal experience,” said Mr. Salih, who as a young man fled repression in Iraq.

The High Commissioner insisted that “while a person may, for a time, be defined as a refugee, becoming a refugee should not define a person’s life.”

He warned that millions of refugees “find themselves trapped in dependency, relying on a dwindling amount of aid for their daily survival.”

Tackling prolonged displacement

Although humanitarian assistance remains indispensable to saving lives during emergencies, too many refugees can spend years, or even decades, in prolonged displacement.

“Being a refugee is meant to be a temporary condition, not a lifelong fate,” he said.

“That is why I have set out an ambitious goal: to cut by half, within ten years, the number of refugees living in protracted displacement and reliant on humanitarian assistance.”

Efforts will focus on low and middle-income countries that host the majority of refugees.

“Achieving this target…would vastly improve the lives of millions of people. It is how we can move from merely managing displacement to resolving it,” he said.

Landmark anniversary

This year’s observation of World Refugee Day also marks the 75th anniversary of the Refugee Convention.

The 1951 treaty, adopted in the aftermath of the Second World War, enshrined that anyone forced to flee war, conflict, or persecution has the right to seek safety and protection.

“We must continue to uphold that promise. Until everyone is safe, none of us are safe,” said Mr. Salih.

“This is not merely a statement of solidarity but a call to action. Because the right to seek safety was made for times like these, and it is up to all of us to defend it.”

Stand up for refugees

The High Commissioner heads the UN refugee agency, UNHCR, which is rallying young people to stand with refugees and defend asylum under the World Refugee Day theme Until Everyone is Safe.

The campaign challenges stereotypes about refugees and emphasises that the right to seek safety goes beyond merely escaping war or violence.

It also complements the goal to reduce the number of refugees in long-term displacement by half by 2035 – first outlined in UNHCR’s recent Global Trends Report.

The 50 by 35 vision aims to boost refugees’ access to work opportunities, national education, health, and social protection systems in efforts to foster self-reliance and reduce dependency on aid.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant global humanitarian issue—supporting refugees—and outlines concrete UN goals to reduce prolonged displacement and improve refugees' lives. It emphasizes solidarity, rights, and actionable targets with broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: UN calls for stronger support to help millions of refugees rebuild their lives
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year on June 20, the world observes World Refugee Day to raise awareness about people forced to leave their homes because of war, violence, or persecution. Today, nearly 42 million people live as refugees, seeking safety in new countries.

**What happened** – The United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), led by Barham Salih, urged countries to increase support for refugees. He highlighted how refugees contribute to their new communities as workers, students, and leaders. UN Secretary-General António Guterres also called for renewed action and solidarity, especially as conflicts continue to force many to flee.

**Impact** – The UN aims to cut in half the number of refugees living in long-term displacement within the next ten years. This goal focuses on helping refugees become more independent by improving access to jobs, education, and healthcare. It marks a shift from just providing emergency aid toward solving the root problems of displacement. This year also marks the 75th anniversary of the Refugee Convention, a treaty that guarantees the right to seek safety.

**What's next step** – UNHCR will work closely with countries, especially those with fewer resources but that host many refugees, to create better opportunities and reduce reliance on aid. Young people worldwide are encouraged to support refugees and help change negative stereotypes as part of a global campaign.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is pushing for stronger global support to help millions of refugees rebuild their lives and reduce long-term dependence on aid by 2035.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU appelle à un soutien renforcé pour aider des millions de réfugiés à reconstruire leur vie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, le 20 juin, la Journée mondiale des réfugiés sensibilise sur les personnes contraintes de fuir leur pays à cause de la guerre, de la violence ou de la persécution. Aujourd’hui, près de 42 millions de personnes vivent en tant que réfugiés, cherchant la sécurité dans de nouveaux pays.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Haut-Commissariat des Nations Unies pour les réfugiés (HCR), dirigé par Barham Salih, a appelé les pays à renforcer leur soutien aux réfugiés. Il a souligné comment les réfugiés contribuent à leurs nouvelles communautés en tant que travailleurs, étudiants et leaders. Le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a également appelé à une action renouvelée et à la solidarité, alors que les conflits continuent de forcer beaucoup à fuir.

**Impact** – L’ONU vise à réduire de moitié dans les dix prochaines années le nombre de réfugiés vivant dans un déplacement prolongé. Cet objectif vise à aider les réfugiés à devenir plus indépendants en améliorant l’accès à l’emploi, à l’éducation et aux soins de santé. Cela marque un passage de l’aide d’urgence à la résolution des causes profondes du déplacement. Cette année marque aussi le 75e anniversaire de la Convention de Genève sur les réfugiés, un traité garantissant le droit de chercher protection.

**Prochaine étape** – Le HCR travaillera étroitement avec les pays, surtout ceux à faibles ressources mais qui accueillent beaucoup de réfugiés, pour créer de meilleures opportunités et réduire la dépendance à l’aide. Les jeunes du monde entier sont encouragés à soutenir les réfugiés et à changer les stéréotypes négatifs dans le cadre d’une campagne mondiale.

**Phrase clé** – L’ONU pousse pour un soutien mondial renforcé afin d’aider des millions de réfugiés à reconstruire leur vie et réduire la dépendance à l’aide d’ici 2035.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU pide un mayor apoyo para ayudar a millones de refugiados a reconstruir sus vidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, el 20 de junio, se celebra el Día Mundial del Refugiado para crear conciencia sobre las personas que deben abandonar sus hogares debido a guerras, violencia o persecución. Actualmente, casi 42 millones de personas viven como refugiados, buscando seguridad en nuevos países.

**Qué pasó** – El Alto Comisionado de las Naciones Unidas para los Refugiados (ACNUR), dirigido por Barham Salih, pidió a los países que aumenten el apoyo a los refugiados. Destacó cómo los refugiados contribuyen a sus nuevas comunidades como trabajadores, estudiantes y líderes. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, también hizo un llamado a una acción renovada y solidaridad ante los continuos conflictos.

**Impacto** – La ONU tiene como objetivo reducir a la mitad en los próximos diez años el número de refugiados en desplazamiento prolongado. Esta meta busca ayudar a los refugiados a ser más independientes mejorando el acceso a empleos, educación y salud. Esto representa un cambio de solo brindar ayuda de emergencia a resolver las causas del desplazamiento. Este año también se celebra el 75º aniversario de la Convención sobre Refugiados, un tratado que garantiza el derecho a buscar protección.

**Próximo paso** – ACNUR trabajará junto a países, especialmente los de ingresos bajos y medios que acogen a la mayoría de refugiados, para crear mejores oportunidades y reducir la dependencia de la ayuda. Se invita a los jóvenes de todo el mundo a apoyar a los refugiados y cambiar estereotipos negativos como parte de una campaña global.

**Frase clave** – La ONU impulsa un apoyo global más fuerte para ayudar a millones de refugiados a reconstruir sus vidas y reducir la dependencia de la ayuda para 2035.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant, interconnected mosaic of hands weaving colorful threads into a strong fabric, symbolizing refugees contributing as workers, students, artists, and leaders; the fabric stretches across a calm landscape with open doors and bridges in soft, natural tones, representing solidarity, opportunity, and the journey from displacement to self-reliance.

articles.title: Old and new challenges for the Human Rights Council as it turns 20
articles.summary: It’s 20 years to the day since the UN Human Rights Council began its work as the world’s principal forum tasked with promoting and defending fundamental rights everywhere, particularly the world’s most vulnerable people.
articles.content:
“Human rights were built for moments like this,” said Awa Dabo, the newly appointed Deputy High Commissioner for Human Rights, “when rights have come under pressure, when people need protection and principles must turn into action.”

Created to replace the UN Commission on Human Rights, the 47 Member States of the new Council were urged to avoid “political point-scoring or petty manoeuvre” – the words of then Secretary-General Kofi Annan on 19 June 2006.

Now, in its 62nd session in Geneva, the Council is busier than ever as it pushes for accountability in many new emergencies and unresolved crises.

But, has it matched up to expectations? And has it adapted to a world where “human rights are under assault [and] violated flagrantly?”, as UN chief António Guterres underscored in a special event on Friday marking two decades of the Council.

‘It was not easy’

“Building the Council was not an easy task,” recalls Luis Alfonso de Alba Góngora, its first president.

“Member States have very different views of what needed to be changed and what needed to be retained from the old Commission [on Human Rights].

It was not easy because the international context situation was not good either; there were countries that opposed the creation of the Human Rights Council and were not supporting the building of the new institution. There were conflicts in Gaza, as today, conflicts in Lebanon…It was not easy.”

Every voice counts

In common with other UN bodies, one of the Council’s aims at its creation was to include as wide a range of participants in its discussions, from governmental to non-governmental speakers and from independent investigators to civil society activists.

Tweet URL

This level of participation “makes sure that Indigenous Peoples are represented” among others whose voices struggle to be heard, explained Volker Türk, High Commissioner for Human Rights.

“I have been to many interactive panels that involve children and young people, but that involve also survivors and victims. That’s a model of participation that the UN needs to stand for and needs to even go further on.”

Council-watchers will often hear the president, who’s appointed on a rotating basis from regional blocs for a year, reminding delegates gently and often that they should be respectful towards each other.

The remark serves as a reminder of the lethal reprisals facing many human rights activists face today and of the key role the forum plays in raising their concerns.

Special investigators

Another key feature of the Council is its human rights investigators who are mandated to monitor situations of concern and report back to raise awareness and encourage action.

There are around 50 Special Rapporteurs who are the “frontline for voices that are unheard”, maintained Farida Shaheed, Special Rapporteur on the right to education. “We also do speak when others fall silent, and sometimes people don’t like what we say. We’re bringing to the table things that are not always discussed.”

Considered approach

The Council has higher status than its predecessor as a subsidiary body of the UN General Assembly. It meets in three regular sessions a year and every time it does so, there are usually two dozen or more resolutions to consider on everything from advancing human rights in specific countries of concern such as South Sudan to freedom of religion or belief and the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment.

Most of these texts detailing what actions the Council expects are adopted without a vote at the end of each session. Some undergo revisions before being approved while others can be withdrawn from the final list. Sometimes this happens following hushed-tones informal negotiations near the UN’s many coffee machines as caffeine-starved delegates dash out from the more plush, carpeted surroundings of the iconic Room XX in Geneva, which is the Council’s home.

When agreement on an issue can’t be reached, such as on Ukraine, a vote is called and the resolution is either adopted or not. And while the Council continues to face accusations of anti-Israeli bias – a nation that remains on its agenda 20 years on – defenders of the forum point to its human rights peer-review of each UN Member State, which happens every four and a half years.

© UN Photo/Jean-Marc Ferré

Funding crunch

Although human rights are one of the UN’s three pillars along with peace and security and development, the current UN-wide funding crisis represents an existential challenge to the work of the Council.

Delegates have less time to speak and interpretation has had to be cut back.

Echoing concerns about the dire financial situation expressed by the High Commissioner for Human Rights, independent experts have also frequently highlighted how a lack of funding has forced them to rationalise their workload.

Heady days

Over and above the challenges facing the Council, senior communications official Rolando Gomez remembers the excitement of the first session back in 2006.

“As part of the small media team supporting the launch, there was a real sense that we were witnessing the beginning of something new, a renewed commitment by the international community to strengthen the UN’s work on human rights and give these issues a more prominent place on the global agenda.

“While it has faced its share of challenges and controversies, it has become an indispensable forum for dialogue, accountability and action on human rights issues worldwide,” he said.

“What has always struck me most is its ability to provide a unique stage for voices that are often not heard elsewhere — victims, human rights defenders, independent experts and civil society representatives whose testimonies bring human rights realities into sharp focus.”

Also in the Council at the very beginning was veteran political counsellor Bob Last from the UK Mission “On day one there was a mixture of excitement and hope. Excitement because we were starting something big and with a chance to build a new institution, something not many of us who work at the UN ever get to do.

“And hope because this was a chance to improve on the failings of the Commission on Human Rights. There was, and still is, disagreement on what those failings were. But on that first day, [former UN Secretary-General] Kofi Annan was very clear on what he saw as the Commission’s failings, why he had called for its replacement and his hopes for the Council.”

Watch then UN Secretary-General Annan’s historic address at Council’s inaugural meeting in 2006:

articles.reasoning: The article highlights the 20-year milestone of the UN Human Rights Council, emphasizing its significant role in promoting and defending fundamental human rights globally, including for vulnerable populations. It details the Council's inclusive participation, accountability mechanisms, and ongoing challenges, providing substantial context on its positive impact and importance as an international institution.
articles.personality_title: UN Human Rights Council marks 20 years of defending rights worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The UN Human Rights Council started 20 years ago to replace an older group, aiming to protect basic human rights around the world, especially for people who are most vulnerable. It was created to be a more effective and fair forum for discussing and acting on human rights issues.

**What happened** – On its 20th anniversary, the Council held its 62nd session in Geneva. It brings together 47 member countries and many other voices, including activists, victims, and experts, to address current human rights problems. The Council works by listening to different opinions, investigating concerns, and passing resolutions to encourage action.

**Impact** – Over two decades, the Council has become a key place where difficult human rights topics are discussed openly. It gives a platform to people who usually do not have a voice, such as Indigenous peoples and victims of abuse. The Council’s special investigators help bring attention to urgent problems worldwide. Despite challenges like political disagreements and funding shortages, it continues to push for accountability and protection.

**What’s next step** – The Council will keep meeting regularly to respond to new crises and review human rights situations in all UN member countries every four and a half years. It faces ongoing challenges like limited funds and political tensions, but its role in promoting dialogue and action remains vital.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For 20 years, the UN Human Rights Council has provided a global stage where diverse voices come together to defend and promote human rights despite many challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Conseil des droits de l’homme de l’ONU fête 20 ans de défense des droits dans le monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Conseil des droits de l’homme de l’ONU a été créé il y a 20 ans pour remplacer une ancienne commission, avec pour mission de protéger les droits fondamentaux, notamment ceux des populations les plus vulnérables. Il devait offrir un forum plus efficace et équitable pour discuter et agir sur les questions de droits humains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour son 20e anniversaire, le Conseil a tenu sa 62e session à Genève. Il réunit 47 États membres ainsi que de nombreux autres participants, comme des activistes, des victimes et des experts, pour traiter des problèmes actuels liés aux droits humains. Le Conseil écoute différentes opinions, enquête sur les préoccupations et adopte des résolutions pour encourager l’action.

**Impact** – En deux décennies, le Conseil est devenu un lieu clé où sont discutés ouvertement des sujets difficiles. Il donne une voix à ceux qui sont souvent ignorés, comme les peuples autochtones et les victimes d’abus. Ses experts spéciaux attirent l’attention sur des urgences mondiales. Malgré des tensions politiques et un manque de financement, il continue à promouvoir la responsabilité et la protection.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Conseil continuera à se réunir régulièrement pour répondre aux nouvelles crises et examiner la situation des droits dans tous les pays membres de l’ONU tous les quatre ans et demi. Il doit relever des défis comme les fonds limités et les tensions politiques, mais son rôle dans le dialogue et l’action reste essentiel.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Depuis 20 ans, le Conseil des droits de l’homme de l’ONU offre une scène mondiale où diverses voix se réunissent pour défendre et promouvoir les droits humains malgré de nombreux défis.
articles.personality_title_es: El Consejo de Derechos Humanos de la ONU cumple 20 años defendiendo los derechos en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Consejo de Derechos Humanos de la ONU comenzó hace 20 años para reemplazar a un grupo anterior, con el objetivo de proteger los derechos básicos de las personas en todo el mundo, especialmente de los más vulnerables. Fue creado para ser un foro más efectivo y justo para discutir y actuar sobre temas de derechos humanos.

**Qué pasó** – En su 20 aniversario, el Consejo celebró su 62ª sesión en Ginebra. Reúne a 47 países miembros y a muchas otras voces, como activistas, víctimas y expertos, para abordar problemas actuales de derechos humanos. El Consejo escucha diferentes opiniones, investiga preocupaciones y aprueba resoluciones para fomentar la acción.

**Impacto** – Durante dos décadas, el Consejo se ha convertido en un lugar clave donde se discuten abiertamente temas difíciles de derechos humanos. Da una plataforma a personas que normalmente no tienen voz, como pueblos indígenas y víctimas de abusos. Los investigadores especiales del Consejo ayudan a llamar la atención sobre problemas urgentes en el mundo. A pesar de desafíos como desacuerdos políticos y falta de fondos, sigue impulsando la responsabilidad y la protección.

**Próximo paso** – El Consejo continuará reuniéndose regularmente para responder a nuevas crisis y revisar la situación de los derechos humanos en todos los países miembros de la ONU cada cuatro años y medio. Enfrenta desafíos continuos como fondos limitados y tensiones políticas, pero su papel en promover el diálogo y la acción sigue siendo vital.

**Resumen en una frase** – Durante 20 años, el Consejo de Derechos Humanos de la ONU ha proporcionado un escenario global donde diversas voces se unen para defender y promover los derechos humanos a pesar de muchos desafíos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, ancient stone circle set on a gentle hill under a calm sky, each stone engraved with faint silhouettes of diverse voices rising upward as glowing threads connecting them, symbolizing global dialogue and unity, while a warm, soft light envelops the scene, representing hope and ongoing commitment to human rights across time.
